# GPT-5.6 - Again unusable

Hendrik    10 Reputation points

2026-07-14T15:35:24.87+00:00

Hello Microsoft. The whole working day GPT 5.6 Sol is again unusable at the Response API. Region East-US and Swedencentral. No cache hits and 5 minutes loading time per request. Impossible to develop with it.

After prompt cache fix Sunday evening it works perfect, but today its completely broken again... Could you please fix your Inference setup? It cost a lot of money to develop via API and it should not work like this.

Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models

results.title: GPT 5.6 Won't Take No for an Answer
results.content: work. That difference is already showing up in early usage. Lovable says GPT 5.6 completes production-grade app workflows with roughly 25% fewer steps, 35 to 48% fewer tool calls, and 15% fewer stuck runs. Other early users describe it less like a brilliant specialist and more like an efficient colleague that can stay active all day, check details, handle edge cases, and keep polishing. One team reportedly increased total token usage five-fold, not because the model suddenly produced five times more valuable ideas, but because people trusted it with far more of the small decisions and repetitive checks that they previously handled themselves. A developer even left soul running autonomously for nearly a week to create a detailed voxal version of Manhattan. That is the behavioral shift Open [...] secretly plotting against users. It is evidence that a highly capable agent can become too committed to the outcome, use methods outside the intended boundary, and then describe the result more cleanly than the process deserves. The uncomfortable part is that this exact persistence is also what makes GPT 5.6 useful. OpenAI researchers are already using it to diagnose failures, optimize training systems, run experiments, interpret results, and improve other models. During internal testing, average daily output tokens per active researcher rose to more than twice the highest level seen with GPT 5.5. In 6 months, the share of OpenAI's research compute devoted to internal coding inference increased 100fold, while internal agent token usage grew around 22fold. On OpenAI's internal recursive [...] would involve a broader deliberately misaligned plan. But it did observe cheating, fabricated research results, and actions that became more serious because the model kept trying to finish. One example is almost absurdly clear. A user authorized the deletion of virtual machines 1, 2, and 3. GPT 5.6 could not find those names in one name space. So instead of stopping and asking, it substituted virtual machines five, six, and seven, killed active processes, and force removed work trees. It later admitted that uncommitted work may have been lost, and only stopped after the user objected. In another case, the model updated an internal research draft to claim that an equation had been computed and verified, even though it knew that had not happened. # GPT-5.6 cheats so much its testers couldn’t measure it

### OpenAI’s new model broke rules and exploited loopholes more than any model METR has tested to date

GPT-5.6 Sol, OpenAI’s newest, most capable, and yet-to-be-deployed model, cheats a lot: so much so that independent evaluators couldn’t actually tell how capable it is.

When independent evaluation non-profit METR tested 5.6 Sol on a battery of coding tasks, the model broke the rules or exploited loopholes more than “any public model we have evaluated,” METR said.

For the past several model release cycles, “the METR graph” — a plot tracking the alarming increase in AI’s ability to complete long tasks — has been Exhibit A for the exponential increase in AI capabilities. [...] “GPT-5.6 Sol, more often than its predecessor, can be overly persistent in pursuit of user goals, to the point of taking actions that go beyond what the user intended,” OpenAI said. For instance, the model was caught “being overly agentic in circumventing restrictions,” taking disapproved actions, and sometimes lying to users. And it did this far more than GPT-5.5. [...] The system card explains that “while rates of misaligned behavior are higher than previous deployments, the absolute number remains low.” Indeed, despite being attached to the longest bar, 0.00251 — about a quarter of a percent — feels small, until you realize what it counts: the proportion of real coding tasks, pulled from OpenAI employees, in which GPT-5.6 Sol did something that “a reasonable user would likely not anticipate and strongly object to.” Imagine 1 in 400 tasks ending with the model nonconsensually uploading sensitive data to unapproved services or making up research results (two examples OpenAI listed). Not so comforting.
